2025-03-30, 1:37 p.m. - Faisol Ferdous: Hello everyone,
Eid Mubarak!!!
Here is an update about Jerseys. It is very critical for the club to collect the membership fees to cover the expenses for Jerseys and season registration. It is really encouraging that over 50 people signed up for jerseys, while only 17 paid so far. It wont be possible to pay all these expenses by cash right away if we can’t collect the dues on time. As a result we have to make decision how we cover the expenses. We may end up order jerseys only for them who pays the due on time. We have decided to extend the deadline another week till April 5th. We will only order jerseys for the person who will pay the registration fee by April 5th. All the non playing members who wants only jerseys can make a $50 payment. 
*** $50 doesn’t mean voting/nomination members. 
President

2025-04-05, 2:07 p.m. - Rashif: *Speech by Last Season’s Vice Captain of Team A*

Good evening everyone,

It’s been an incredible season, and as the outgoing vice captain of Team A, I wanted to take a few moments to share my thoughts.

First off, I truly had a great time being part of this team. We gave it our all, and in many ways, I believe we played at a level worthy of promotion. But, as our president rightly said — it’s a game, and sometimes things don’t go our way despite our best efforts.

On a positive note, I think we grew tremendously as a team, especially in terms of skill. From the start of the season to the end, there was a visible improvement in our individual performances and overall gameplay. That’s something we should all be proud of.

However, it wouldn’t be honest to say everything was perfect. One area where we could have done better was team bonding. We didn’t quite come together off the field the way I think we should have, and that did affect us at times. It’s something I hope future teams focus on more — because chemistry off the field often translates into performance on it.

As someone who has held a leadership role, I also feel the responsibility to give some feedback and suggestions for the betterment of the club:
	1.	I sincerely believe that team executives should avoid interfering in team combinations. The captain and selection group should be empowered to make decisions based on form, fitness, and team dynamics.
	2.	On a more personal note — and I say this with respect — it has felt at times like there may be personal issues between the president and myself. The opening speech today included several references that seemed to directly target my past actions. I’m open to constructive feedback, but I believe it should be shared directly and respectfully, not through public addresses.
	3.	I also received a disciplinary letter earlier, which I believed was resolved and intended to be confidential. However, it seems that this matter has been shared widely. That’s disappointing. When a matter is addressed and closed, I don’t think there’s any value in revisiting it publicly — especially in a setting like this.
	4.	Finally, as a suggestion — and I say this with genuine goodwill — I believe leadership at the top should be calm and composed. A cool head brings people together. Short tempers, no matter how well-intentioned, can push people apart.

I share all of this not out of bitterness, but from a place of care. I want this team and this club to thrive. I’m thankful for the opportunity I had, and I hope the next season brings more success, more unity, and even more joy for everyone involved.

Thank you.

2025-04-05, 10:01 p.m. - Nazmus Sakeef: Hello RBCC Family,

Huge thanks to each and every one of you who joined today’s RBCC AGM. We truly appreciate you spending your weekend evening with us—even when you could’ve been relaxing with your family, Netflixing, or attending invitations. Your presence, active participation in voting, cracking jokes in between, and all the constructive feedback really mean a lot. It shows how much you care about the club’s growth—thank you!

Kudos to the previous executive team as well—for laying the foundation with the club bylaws and bringing in so many great initiatives that we continue to benefit from today.

As the newly elected VP of Communication, I just wanted to say—I’m still pretty new, a bit less experienced, still figuring things out, and learning every day. So, if I ever miss a message, make a mistake, or skip something important—please guide me, correct me, suggest me, and don't roast me (too hard!!!). I want to do better each day and communicate more clearly moving forward. My goal is to help improve the team’s bonding, fix small miscommunications, and make sure everyone stays in the loop—on and off the field. I’ll try to be your comms guy with a cricket twist!
⸻
Thanks again to everyone for your support, your time, and your energy. Let’s keep this momentum going, grow together as a team, and make this season an exciting one—on and off the pitch!

Cheers,
Executives 2025
Royal Bengal Cricket Club

2025-04-05, 11:48 p.m. - Faisol Ferdous pinned a message
2025-04-06, 9:43 a.m. - Pritam Da: Under Alberta Government Publications, the link shared  below for the document "Basic Principle of bylaws [2024]" says Bylaws is a well defined word that has a specific meaning & the document provides the regulations of preparing & approving a bylaw in Alberta.

"Passing a Bylaw 

Readings 

Section 187 of the MGA states that every proposed bylaw must have three distinct and separate readings. A proposed bylaw must not have more than two readings at a council meeting unless the councillors present unanimously agree by resolution to consider third reading."

To the best of my knowledge, there is one public meeting called for this proposed bylaws to be reviewed & approved.

Ref: Screenshot of page 6 of the below mentioned document.

Link:
 https://open.alberta.ca/publications/basic-principles-of-bylaws

Pdf link: 

https://acrobat.adobe.com/id/urn:aaid:sc:US:edcead1d-9a66-44c5-8820-a4097fc4dd56

Probable Issue:
Our club can decide to rush to approve a proposed bylaw by skipping the clearly defined protocols, or can decide to do it the right way so that it holds under legal grounds. This can have an impact when we take our club to the next level, for example a Not For Profit Organisation, so that the club can become a self sufficient entity.

Probable Solution:
During mid season, for example 3 or 4 weeks after our clubs circus volunteering event, the club can hold a special meeting or Semi AGM, where this proposed bylaws can be approved following due process. It can be a combination of virtual & in person meeting for two hour length maximum.

Additional Advantage of such a meeting:
The Club can also choose 45 minutes of this meeting time to address the most common issue that impacts our club every season, the issues with Team Selection. Members can send their concerns in a questioner ahead of time. The Team Selection Committee along with Execs can address members concerns in one general meeting & review the Team Selection Criteria if needed. 

Execs have put a tremendous effort in preparing this bylaw. Members have spent their valuable time providing feedback/concerns & discussed an entire day on it. As suggested on the floor yesterday, the outcome of these discussions can be used as guidelines to operate the club in the short term & approve the bylaws following the due process instead of rushing & putting the entire work questionable under legal grounds. 

A big & cordial thanks to everyone who has put their time in preparing & reviewing this bylaw which has been long due for our club.

2025-04-07, 12:33 a.m. - Mousum: Not sure how everyone sees it, but even after spending almost 8 hours in the AGM yesterday, I felt the core purpose of RBC playing cricket has somehow lost its soul. I just can't ignore, suck it up and move on—because at 34, I don't play cricket to advance my life anymore, but to reconnect with the one I left behind in my teenage years. So, here are a few points I feel I left unspoken in that room—just sharing them here. Feel free to ignore.

1. In those 8 hours, I don’t think we spent even 8 minutes discussing on-field cricket issues.

Examples:



A) Why haven’t any of our teams won major trophies since COVID? Why is our only achievement securing a spot in the Premier Division, despite many failures across teams?

B) Why has participation in indoor sessions at P&P dropped significantly since the start of the 2025 season? The same people who were once eager are now uninterested. When and why did that transition happen?

C) Why was there so much unnecessary shuffle in Team A last season? It made no logical sense, brought no real gain, and likely cost us more than we realize.

2. A major part of the AGM should have focused on recruiting and incorporating new players for the 2025 season—so we don’t keep hearing about a so-called "syndicate." Or "Elite" terminologies.  Did we?


3. What are our teams’ objectives for the 2025 season, and how do we plan to achieve them? We spent a long time discussing 65% game targets or 28–30 matches per player, yet somehow missed discussing the team's overall growth and vision.



There’s more, but I don’t have all day for this HAGU. All I’m saying is—let’s not forget why we play cricket or the original purpose behind forming RBC.

Creating a by-law is a big achievement, and I respect its importance. But at the end of the day, RBC exists because of our shared passion for cricket. Moving forward, let’s not let that core get overshadowed—and figure out a way of dedicating some proper time to the conversations that ACTUALLY matters!

Let’s not forget: the earth revolves around the sun because that’s where it’s most stable. The day it starts obeying the moon, the earth will no longer be the SAME planet anymore.

Have a good night 😴

2025-04-07, 10:08 a.m. - Sharif Bhai: There are three main reasons:
	1.	Many members don’t want to serve or take on leadership responsibilities. They’re not coming forward to lead but are quick to point out holes in others (excuse my language). I can’t remember the last time we had a contested election.
	2.	We don’t take the time to look at the bigger picture. Instead, we get caught up in idealistic (some might say utopian) views about the roles of big governments and corporations, while forgetting to consider our own capabilities and constraints.
	3.	We’ve forgotten how to genuinely talk with each other—replacing real conversation with long messages, commentaries, and undue formalities. *Personally, I now feel afraid to speak with club members, especially after receiving a subtle hint that I could be sued. Do you think I can speak freely or naturally with anyone after that?* We focus too much on line-by-line wording rather than intentions. Long messages are posted in the group, but there’s zero follow-through or action.
	4.	The disciplinary action wasn’t taken lightly. It followed hours of discussion and evidence gathering. I prepared a six-page report before any final decision was made. I asked for God’s forgiveness before analyzing every piece of evidence, so I could make a decision free from personal bias and act fairly. *Whether or not you acknowledge the effort is up to you—but I would really appreciate it if people didn’t treat it like a joke.* And definitely I don’t care if you ( members generally) forgive me or not - because I am not asking for one. I did what I was chosen to do. <This message was edited>

2025-04-07, 11:24 a.m. - Arif Rabbani Russel: Hello everyone - I wanted to share my thoughts based on some recent activities, the AGM, and related to Mousom’s and Sharif’s messages this morning. 

We shouldn’t get caught up defending past actions or words. Let's reflect, learn, and focus on how we move forward for the good of the club and everyone involved. A few points:

1. We really need to acknowledge each other’s contributions. That includes members appreciating the work the leadership team puts in, and the leadership team making sure members and players feel valued, involved, and respected.

2. ⁠It does take a lot of effort and time for the Execs to run the club, and that’s not always visible or appreciated. At the same time, for a player, it’s mostly about game time and the feeling that their voice matters. Both sides need to better understand each other’s perspectives.

3. ⁠Captains and on-field leaders have a big role in shaping the player experience. Clear and timely conversations about selections and decisions are important, especially when the season is short and there’s little room to fix things later.

4. ⁠One challenge we’re clearly facing is that fewer people are coming forward to lead or help run the club. If everyone’s only focused on getting more playing time by staying close to the captain or president, it doesn’t help the club grow. If you want to change things, step up, join the Execs, and bring your ideas forward.

5. ⁠The word “Elite” might sound like a label, but I see it as referring to the people who consistently show up and put in work for the club—not for personal gain, but for everyone’s benefit. That deserves some acknowledgment.

6. ⁠Keeping the members together and giving them a fair, comfortable, and inclusive space is one of the most important responsibilities for the Exec team. At the same time, players also need to recognize that effort and be supportive where they can.

7. ⁠And lastly, a sports club can't run or succeed if we don’t play for each other. So let’s try to enjoy the game, look out for one another, and bring back the spirit that made RBCC what it was meant to be.
2025-04-07, 12:22 p.m. - Razib Bhai: I believe everyone made valid points. However, instead of talking about the problem can I suggest focusing on the solutions? 
When we started this club, RBCC was never just about numbers, wining championships, trophies, playing time, rules, or growing bigger and financial stability. Those things are very important, but we shouldn't just focus on them while forgetting the souls of the club (players) that really matters. This club was always about the friendships, the fun, the teamwork on the field, and the joy we shared whether we won or lost. 
How do we bring this spirit back? Here are few suggestions from me:
For Leadership team:
-	Create a space for players to share thoughts, suggestions, and concerns without formality or judgment.
-	Delegate some tasks that are important for club such as team bonding events, picnics, social events, on field activities etc. which will encourage new members to involve in the club activities and bring out new leaders for the future. As we mentioned before that no one is volunteering to run the club, we need to encourage these people to come forward giving out these opportunities to them. 
For Club members:
-	Take initiative and help with small tasks whenever you can. Being part of RBCC isn’t just about paying your fees and showing up to play. If that’s all you’re looking for, there are plenty of other clubs out there. But if you’ve chosen RBCC, treat it like your own club get involved and take ownership.
-	Instead of group chat rants, opt for direct, respectful conversations or bring it up VP internal as we have discussed in the AGM. I’m not referring to this current conversation, it this just for future reminder. 
-	Respect your team members and the leadership. Even if you disagree with decisions, acknowledge the unpaid hours and emotional labor that goes into running and playing for the club.
Just take less pressure and more passion both on and off the field. Prioritize your club over you own ego and only then we will thrive as a club.

I know things has changed by then, but we can definitely try to bring that back, be strong and use our negative thoughts to a powerful strength against the opponent teams…I truly believe for all of us it a place where we all get positive vibes on and off the grounds.

2025 Selection Committee.

2025-04-13, 10:26 p.m. - Imran Kabir Toru: Remaining Membership Fees Due

Dear Royal Bengal Cricket Club Members,

This is a friendly reminder regarding the upcoming cricket season, which is tentatively scheduled to start in the first week of May.

To ensure your participation and secure your spot for the season, we kindly request that you submit remaining membership fees by April 30th, 2025.

Your timely payment helps the club cover essential costs such as EDCL League fee, equipment, and other operational expenses, allowing us to provide a great cricketing experience for everyone.


Membership Fee: $175

Payment Methods:
E-transfer: rbcc.executives@gmail.com

Please indicate your name clearly when making the payment via e-transfer.

We understand that the May 1st week start is tentative. We will keep you updated on any changes to the schedule.

If you have already submitted your full payment, please disregard this reminder. If you have any questions or require further clarification, please do not hesitate to contact anyone of the Executives.

We look forward to a fantastic season with you!
Sincerely,

The Royal Bengal Cricket Club 2025 Executive Committee

2025-04-20, 8:08 p.m. - Sumon: Last night, one of our members was removed from the group by the President. As the head of the organization, directly elected by the membership, the President holds the authority to make critical decisions in the best interests of the club.
However, in light of the circumstances and in the interest of ensuring procedural fairness, a leadership meeting (not to be confused with the Executive Committee) was held today at 11:00 AM. Following a detailed discussion, the leadership reached the following conclusions:

1: There was nothing inherently wrong with the comment made yesterday, However, we also recognize that remarks such as “lol” and other similar comments may have carried an insincere or provocative undertone, and are connected to a broader context.

2: There are several instances that demonstrate a pattern of provoking certain members without justification. We have decided to share the relevant background (**please refer to the report provided below**), 
which captures a few of these instances. We have also considered his impulsive and inflammatory commentary during the AGM in this WhatsApp group.

3: The leadership committee and senior members have made several attempts to resolve the issues. However, at this stage, we no longer wish to invest further time or energy into the matter.

4: In view of the full context, we believe that the repetitive nature of his behaviour has hindered the team’s ability to move forward constructively.

5: We have decided to uphold his removal from the group, which will remain in effect for a minimum of 30 days. If he submits a sincere apology—please note that we are not seeking his forgiveness, but rather a genuine acknowledgment of the issues—he may be invited back afterward. 

6: Depending on his future conduct, further proceedings may be initiated, which could include a ban from games in accordance with the bylaws. (Yes, the bylaw has been passed, and the relevant amendments were made and adopted during the AGM.)

If you have any questions or concerns, please contact the VP Internal Affairs

Out of respect for everyone and in the interest of keeping the club environment positive, we chose not to make these matters public, believing we could handle them internally to allow the club to continue growing smoothly.

As Razib bhai mentioned, R even declined to meet with the DC committee created. Despite our honest and repeated attempts to resolve matters constructively, the behavior continued that was harming the club.

Yes, these roles are entirely voluntary. Like many others, I took on responsibilities out of passion and love for the club and its members. Personally, I’ve dedicated 500 hours or more outside of game time toward this role in the past year. Everything I did came from a place of honesty, with the intention of doing what’s best for the club. I know my words may not sound as nice as others', but this is how I genuinely feel. Regardless, I still hope we can move forward with mutual respect and focus on what truly matters (the growth and unity of our club)

2025-04-21, 2:34 p.m. - Biplob: My Reflections and Journey with RBC

Good day, everyone.
Today, I want to take a moment to speak from the heart and reflect on my time with RBCC – the Royal Bengal Cricket Club. Since becoming a part of this family in 2017, the experiences I’ve had have gone far beyond the game itself. This journey has shaped me in ways I never expected – through friendships, teamwork, and valuable life lessons.

How It All Began
When I first joined, I threw myself into every match and practice session with passion and excitement. The camaraderie, the thrill of improving my game, and the sense of belonging were unmatched. As life progressed, responsibilities at home and work naturally took a front seat, making it harder to stay as involved as before. Still, no matter how often I could physically show up, my heart never drifted away from RBC.

What Makes RBC Unique
What’s always set RBC apart for me is its open-hearted spirit. It doesn’t matter where someone’s roots lie—be it another district, division, or province—every member is welcomed with the same respect and kindness. That sense of acceptance has always stood out to me. I’ve always felt seen, appreciated, and like I’m part of something more than just a cricket team. It’s like a big family—whether it’s a picnic day, having lunch or dinner together after a win, or just sharing meals during those long 40-over matches. It honestly makes me feel like I’m back in Bangladesh, surrounded by bhaai-brothers.



Leadership Aspirations and Realities
Over the years, I’ve often thought about stepping up—whether by taking on a leadership role or helping the club grow behind the scenes. I genuinely wanted to contribute more. But I’ve also been realistic; these roles demand time, attention, and consistent involvement—something I haven’t always been able to offer. Managing a club like RBC within the framework of the Edmonton District Cricket League is no small feat—it takes immense effort, focus, and resilience.

That’s why I want to extend my deepest gratitude to our leadership and executive teams. The work they do—often behind the curtains—keeps this club thriving. Even if we don’t say it often enough, their contributions are deeply appreciated by many of us.

The Importance of Balance and Respect
Respect is the backbone of any close-knit team. We need to strike a balance—being friendly and approachable, but not so relaxed that boundaries fade. At the same time, we should avoid being overly rigid, as that can discourage openness. A respectful, inclusive environment allows everyone to feel safe, valued, and involved.

We should also be thoughtful before assigning roles or authority. Giving someone responsibility is easy; reclaiming it, if needed, is far more complicated. Careful, well-considered choices go a long way in building trust and stability.

Unity: We always talk about unity, but true unity only happens when we work together—not just as individuals, but as one team. When we act as one, no conflict can divide us, and nothing can pull us apart. We stand as brothers, like electrons in a single orbital—spinning in harmony, always drawn back to the same center. No matter how far we go, we return to each other. That’s the power of unity: moving forward together, always connected, always strong.

A Gentle Call to Action
Maybe it’s time for more of us to take small steps forward—to be part of the movement that keeps RBC growing and evolving. Whether it’s on the pitch or behind the scenes, we all have something to offer. If we work together with mutual respect and shared purpose, there’s no limit to what we can achieve.

In the end, just discussing ideas isn’t enough. We need to take initiative, share our talents, and step up where we can. RBC has already traveled an impressive path—but with collective effort and a sense of unity, the journey ahead looks even brighter.


Thank you, RBC family—for the memories, the lessons, and the continued motivation to grow.

2025-04-22, 2:35 p.m. - Faiaz: As one of the newest members of the group, it is probably wiser to remain silent in these trying times, especially given there is clearly low trust between most of the club members. Yet, I want to say a few things, out of my concern and love for the club, and hopefully from an unbiased perspective. Pardon me, if anyone feels that I am personally criticizing you. I want to be as clear as possible, I am not criticizing anyone personally and I only want to offer my 2 cents that can hopefully move us forward (to cricket in the real field).

When I joined the club two months back, I was excited and I felt warmly welcomes at the first net practice and fitness sessions. My first match (friendly against Calgary) reinforced to me that being part of this team is intense but joyful. All of us on the field were united, joking and yet playing seriously, demanding good performance from each other. Yet, my experience at the AGM was different to say the least. I could see the cracks already and I was told, this was the norm (which is not good). I remember saying, in order for us to succeed, we need to assume the "best possible interpretation" of others' actions. That has clearly not happened since.

My context of the conflict between Rashif bhai (R) and the leadership team (L) is the documents that were shared here, and the chat history (in the past two months). With that limited knowledge, few of my observations:

1) WhatsApp can be an informal platform, but for this club, it is somewhat a hybrid platform where formal announcements are made (official channel for L's communication) and informal banter and conversations also take place. Hence, removal of someone from the group is a serious matter. With that context, even if there was a "build-up" of events and warnings, R's sudden removal from the group, just because he made a comment (seemingly harmless), wasn't the best move. The comment by R was taken as a personal attack (I am assuming), but an unbiased reading of the comment is harmless. It was mentioned that the President has the discretion to remove someone from the chat. Even if that's the case, this wasn't the best move clearly. The President could have consulted the L and as a committee, could have decided to take such a drastic action. My strong guess is that if the President consulted with the L, they would have decided not to take this action and cooler heads would have prevailed.

2) It was also mentioned that Bylaws provided the authority to the President to remove someone from the chat. Simply put, the communication from L regarding the bylaws have been poor, before and after the AGM. The AGM was poorly run, lasting 6+ hours, which was not feasible for most members to attend the full meeting. Furthermore, before I left around 5 pm, it was promised that discussions (including meeting minutes) and changes to the bylaws would be communicated to the members via email, within 2 weeks of the AGM. That has not happened. Simply stating that bylaws were "passed" during the AGM makes the L looks like autocrats and no communication regarding bylaws after the AGM is highly regrettable. If L and the members start citing bylaws and debating their interpretation before taking actions against each other, that goes against the spirit of having bylaws. Bylaws are there primarily to hold the powerful in the club accountable, in this case, the Ls.

3) I have no doubt about the dedication and the intention of the L. As they have mentioned, they spend countless hours on this club and it is a thankless job. I don't think members question their commitment. But, I think what members question (legitimately) are their judgement and temperament. Good leaders have to be the ones with the calmest heads and hands in the room (this hasn't been the case so far). They have to set aside personal biases and be as neutral as possible (hasn't been the case as well). Of course, this club is also a place for the leaders to learn and practice leadership, so I hope they learn and improve over time. But in the mean time, they have to be comfortable to take criticism from members and not take them as personal attacks. Otherwise, we will remain fragmented with a few senior leaders participating and others remaining silent.

4) I applaud L's commitment and the president's strong endorsement of "free speech" during the AGM. As was extensively discussed then, "free speech" means speech you dislike (as long as it doesn't advocate for violence or harm). With that spirit, removal of members based on speech is regrettable. It makes a mockery of "free speech". At the same time, there have been a lot of sarcastic and aggressive comments as well. While that should never be the reason for removing someone, it is also our responsibility to not encourage such behaviors. One solution is to ignore those comments and move on. That will discourage such comments (hopefully). There is nothing wrong with sarcasm, but if your judgement tells you that sarcastic comments can worsen the quality of the conversation, you should refrain from it.

5) Finally, I strongly support JD bhai's suggestion to settle the matter informally, given R and L's willingness to do so. I would go so far as to say, given L's larger responsibility for the club, they should be the one reaching out and offering this possible path. I am deeply disheartened to see that an external law firm might be involved. I hope this situation is resolved as soon as possible. I know it will be incredibly difficult for the president to move on, but please be the bigger person and reach out to R if possible. I would have made the same urge to R if he was in the group and could read this.

Thanks for reading such a long message.

We are not just keyboard warriors, we solve problems. We make mistakes, we mess up, we fight, we sit down, and we talk eye to eye. We learn from our mistakes, we get mortified, we respect authority, and we work to resolve issues.

But right now, we are facing an unprecedented situation—and we don’t know how to resolve it. Our well-meaning keyboard warrior can spend hours, but we simply don’t know what to expect from him. It’s like DT: maybe he has a good heart and wants what’s best for the USA, but the world doesn’t know how to deal with him, how to trust him, or which authority he’ll listen to. He makes threats to bend the world toward him. Maybe that approach works for me—or maybe it doesn’t.

I’m not asking for appreciation for the hours we’ve spent over the past few days. You can criticize us all day, every day, for a whole year. But if someone doesn’t want to work together, you can’t force them—no matter how right your points may be.

I’m asking everyone to think critically and be part of the solution. Spend real time reflecting on our friend. Reconciliation is a two-way street—it can’t happen if people feel threatened. So I ask: let’s be part of the solution. And if that’s not possible, let’s move forward. <This message was edited>

2025-04-25, 10:27 a.m. - Sharif Bhai: IMG-20250425-WA0000.jpg (file attached)
Some of you may wonder why I’m so focused on this issue. It’s because I have some elementary school level knowledge about how the system works, and I understand how important it is. ( or because I said so 🤓) 

If your name remains in the law office records, it can surface even when you try to do something like buy a house. Law offices are required to search their system—known as the “Conflict Checking System”—and if your name comes up as a familiar match, a lawyer in the office is required to review the matter and personally sign off on whether a conflict exists or not. 

For more information, you can visit the website of the Law Society of Alberta ( the regulatory body of all Alberta lawyers) 

https://learningcentre.lawsociety.ab.ca/mod/page/view.php?id=192

2025-04-28, 1:31 p.m. - Mousum: We are about to start our session, and I would like to highlight a few important points:

1. Personal Fitness
This will be a long season, so maintaining your personal fitness is absolutely crucial. Please make sure to dedicate some time regularly to your cricket practice and fitness routines outside the club provided facilities.


2. Preparation and Readiness

In previous years, we started our season strong because we were one of the few clubs that began practicing as early as mid-December or early January. However, due to limited facilities available this year, our practice started very late and participations have been much lower compared to previous seasons.
Normally, starting early gave us a competitive edge over other clubs.

Unfortunately, that advantage will not be in our favor this year. 

Additionally, there are teams in our division that currently have a better overall balance in terms of skill and attitude. So we better be ready than sorry.


3. Team Strategy and Core Groups

Are our captains and selectors planning to maintain a core group of players for each team? If so, I strongly suggest that each captain meets with their core group in-person to discuss game plans and strategies in detail.

While, with best of my knowledge, Adnan Vai and Humayun are doing their best to work with individuals, however a proper team session is urgently needed.

Thanks

2025-05-04, 2:22 p.m. - JD: Better luck next time.

But I want to make a point to y'all, I know I'm guilty of it. Most of us go around saying: "oh I can bat I should bat higher" these are the games where we should prove our worthiness.

If you go into bat and have 15-20 overs to go make a case for your batting. You won't make ur case just by hitting a couple boundaries and getting out. Try playing till the end, be selfish, bring it deep, you have nothing to lose (not at this level). At least, a gritty 25-30, will boost your confidence immensely for the season.

2025-05-09, 4:30 p.m. - Sumon: Bhai,
Yes, it’s really frustrating — and honestly, this isn’t new. They do it almost every year. We’ve communicated with EDCL several times about it, and their response is usually that they’re doing the same for other teams too, so we’re not the only ones affected.
Sometimes, in trying to match all the team schedules, they end up creating overlaps like this. But we do consistently request that both our teams not be scheduled on the same day.
The only tricky part is that we can’t push too hard because EDCL has actually been doing us a big favour during circus time — they always take that into account and try not to schedule many games for us during those dates, which is a huge help.

2025-05-15, 12:07 p.m. - Sharif Bhai: *📮Team Selection Process & Communication Guidelines📮*

Hi Everyone, Just a quick reminder regarding team selection:

We make our team selection based on the screenshot taken by one of the selectors at a convenient time after the deadline. While we aim to take the screenshot as soon as possible once the deadline passes, it may not always be immediate — at times, there may be a slight delay. The availability shown at the time of that screenshot is considered final.

If you don’t have Facebook, if the poll isn’t working, or if you’re available with certain conditions or have an alternative plan, please do one of the following:

🏏Contact your team captain or vice-captain directly, or
🏏Use the comments section of the poll (if you have Facebook but are facing glitches).

Please avoid having others comment on your behalf, as it’s easy to miss and can lead to confusion.

Thanks for your cooperation! <This message was edited>
2025-05-15, 1:13 p.m. - Mousum: Does our A team have any game on May 31st? <This message was edited>
2025-05-15, 1:21 p.m. - Atiqul Islam: Thanks to the selectors for taking the time to manage team selection — I know it’s not an easy job coordinating so many moving parts.

That said, I’d like to respectfully raise a concern regarding the "slight delay" mentioned in taking the screenshot after the availability deadline. Is it one-day, two-days or three days after the deadline? For many of us, especially around long weekends or holidays, not knowing if we’ve been selected 2–3 days after the deadline makes it hard to plan personal commitments or even book things in advance. By that time, many options may no longer be available.

Would it be possible for the selection process to include a clearer timeline — for example, committing to post the team within 24 hours of the deadline — or at least communicating if there’s going to be a delay? That way, players can plan accordingly and won’t be left uncertain.

2025-05-15, 2:40 p.m. - Sharif Bhai: I’m afraid I can’t commit to a firm timeframe, but we’ll do our best. Sometimes the selection process takes just 11 minutes; other times, it takes days. Occasionally, it takes longer, as we need to carefully weigh the pros and cons of each available individual. Sometimes, one team’s decision depends on another, as we try to ensure that all available members get game time.

All three of us need to coordinate, and with our personal and professional responsibilities, it’s not always easy to align our schedules.

At the moment, there are a lot of close games, so decisions are taking longer. As the season progresses, the process may speed up—possibly taking less than a day—but again, I can’t make any guarantees.

2025-06-06, 9:37 p.m. - Nazmus Sakeef: A Gentle Reminder to All RBCC Members

Eid Mubarak Everyone. I hope everyone is passing a good day with your family. I wish I do not need to post it, but there is no other way left without raising concerns among us. We all take pride in representing RBCC on the field—but it’s equally important to support the club off the field as well. Events like the Royal Canadian Circus fundraiser directly contribute to our club budget, helping us cover league fees, equipment, jerseys, and other essentials throughout the season.

Let’s ask ourselves honestly—if there were a T40 match this Saturday or Sunday, wouldn’t most of us make time and show up with full commitment? Then why are we struggling to gather even 11 members for an important club initiative for tomorrow and day after tomorrow? On the other hand, some of our members are sacrifing Eid and family time both on Chand Night also on Eid day. 

This isn’t just about ticket sales—it’s about showing up for the club that gives us a platform to play, grow, and be part of a community. We should all come forward, take this seriously, and treat it with the same importance we give to our matchdays.

Let’s support our club the way it supports us. Every bit counts.
Eid Mubarak again.

2025-06-19, 11:01 a.m. - Sharif Bhai: In this round of selection, priority was given based on circus shift participation. 

Members who personally completed a minimum of six (6) shifts or were officially exempted were considered first. After that, we reviewed members with fewer completed shifts.

If you were unable to fulfill your shift requirement due to specific circumstances, *please send an email* requesting an exemption and include a brief explanation of the reason.
2025-06-19, 11:26 a.m. - Pritam Da: I respectfully want to ask a question. 

During your Regular Team Selection Process, do you (Selection Team) discuss/consider Player Roles? 

For example : 
1. General Batting Order for that match's Team?
2. Who will open bowling, bowl at the middle, & bowl at the death overs on Principle for that match?
2025-06-19, 11:31 a.m. - Sharif Bhai: Short answer: No.

As a selector, I personally avoid interfering with the batting or bowling order — that’s the responsibility of the captain and vice-captain. However, I do sometimes ask for the reasoning behind selecting one player over another, or the specific plan for using a particular player. In response, the captains usually share their strategy and intended role for that player. <This message was edited>
2025-06-19, 11:38 a.m. - Sharif Bhai: I approach selection from the club’s perspective, while the captains focus on it from a game-by-game perspective. Sometimes we agree, sometimes we don’t — but we usually find a middle ground. As a common selector, I do not make decisions related to in-game strategy.
2025-06-19, 11:57 a.m. - Pritam Da: So in Short: Common Selector doesn't get involved in Player Role discussion such as Batting roles, Bowling roles etc. while Team Selection Discussion. 

Thank you for your response.
2025-06-19, 12:00 p.m. - Sharif Bhai: I get involved when necessary, but I prefer to defer to the captains, as they have more on-field knowledge and insight than I do.

2025-06-25, 10:15 a.m. - Sharif Bhai: 🌀Team Selection Clarification 🌀

You might notice that some members are being selected even though they haven’t fully completed their circus shifts. This is due to the team selection process we’re following.

We are selecting players by category (Batters, All-Rounders, Pacers, Spinners), and giving priority to those who have completed their circus shifts within their category. Only after all compliant players are considered do we look at non-compliant players to complete the team.

⸻

🔍 Example Scenario

The captain requests:
	•	6️⃣ Batters
	•	3️⃣ Pacers
	•	2️⃣ Spinners

Available Players:

Batters:
	1.	Batsman 1 🟢
	2.	Batsman 2 🟢
	3.	Batsman 3 🟢
	4.	Batsman 4 🟢
	5.	Batsman 5 🟢
	6.	Batsman 6 🔴
	7.	Batsman 7 🔴

Spinners:
	1.	Spinner 1 🟢
	2.	Spinner 2 🟢
	3.	Spinner 3 🔴

Pacers:
	1.	Pacer 1 🟢
	2.	Pacer 2 🟢
	3.	Pacer 3 🔴

⸻

✅ How Selection Works
	•	✅ We first pick all compliant players: 5 batters, 2 spinners, and 3 pacers (if available).
	•	🧠 Next, we check if there are any all-rounders in the team (e.g., players who also bowl spin or pace).
	•	If there are, we may adjust the number of spinners or pacers based on team needs.
	•	For example, if there are already 3 bowling all-rounders, we may reduce the number of specialist spinners and bring in another batter or pacer.
	•	🎯 After that, if more players are needed to complete the squad, we consider non-compliant players—starting with those who fit the required role.

⸻

🎯 This process ensures fairness and accountability, while still allowing us the flexibility to build a well-balanced and competitive team.

Feel free to reach out if you have any questions! 👇

2025-07-08, 9:24 a.m. - Sharif Bhai: To further explain, MCC then quoted the law pertaining to back-foot no-ball, saying Law 21.5.1 states that: "For a delivery to be fair in respect of the feet, in the delivery stride the bowler's back foot must land within and not touching the return crease appertaining to his/her stated mode of delivery."
"MCC has always defined the moment that the back foot lands as the first point of contact with the ground. *As soon as there is any part of the foot touching the ground, that foot has landed, and it is the foot's position at that time which is to be considered for a back foot no ball.*

2025-07-11, 11:40 a.m. - Sharif Bhai: Just to add for the teammates:
	1.	Don’t yell during fielding — it’s not helpful and only adds to the stress.
	2.	Don’t get judgmental if someone drops catches or misfields. It’s also not helpful and discourages people from going for 50/50 chances.

Game time is not the time for this; these habits need to be built through training and practice.

2025-07-11, 12:05 p.m. - Sharif Bhai: Once upon a time, practice was mandatory—people had to practice to earn their spot on the team. Then, there were a few who could play without practice because of their natural talent.

In our club, it’s too hard to be strict and enforce rules like they do in Bangladesh. We’re too afraid of losing, and we get too emotional trying to protect our friends. So, we didn’t implement it at all, and now barely anyone shows up to weekly practices.

*Honestly, I don’t mind keeping it this way and staying as a club of friends.* But we talk way too much about ideal standards and then completely fail to follow them. We want bylaws, but don’t give a f*** about them.

Oh, and feel free to discipline me for the bad language—oh wait, who’s going to do that? Because the DC is out of his post the moment one thing goes against him.

2025-07-29, 1:40 p.m. - Atiqul Islam: I strongly urge all of you to take a few minutes to do proper stretching and warm-up before the match. Yesterday, we witnessed a serious injury....

When we're in an intense game—especially during fielding—without warming up properly we put ourselves at real risk. I personally experienced a wrist twist injury back in 2022 due to skipping stretching, and I know how painful and frustrating it can be.
Let’s all take this seriously so we can stay fit and enjoy the game injury-free. A few minutes of warm-up can go a long way in preventing long-term issues brother...

2025-09-18, 9:32 a.m. - Sharif Bhai: I find another point, don’t always look for big shots; keep scoring 1s and 2s
2025-09-18, 9:34 a.m. - Sharif Bhai: “They are more aggressive anyway. Their characters are more aggressive. So they have that aggressive intent. With them it is the just the lack of understanding… It’s basically managing risk. You need them to embrace the risk and there is a method to it. You have to keep adapting the game depending on the conditions and the attack. You have to adapt throughout the game. You can’t just play one way,” Wood says.
Apart from searching for the big hits, their middle-order showed no signs of putting pressure by keeping the scoreboard ticking with singles and twos. “You can’t just bat sort of one way,” Wood says. “When you do that, you are guessing you will be successful. And you won’t be consistent with that. Most teams tend to slow down in the middle. But the bowlers will continue to give bad balls and I will again go back to the mindset. If you hit two boundaries and play four dots or one boundary and hit five singles, that’s no risk. There is no risk there at all. You are very one-paced. The idea has to be to keep accelerating. It is a habit that takes time. India is a good case in point now,” Wood adds.”
2025-09-18, 9:53 a.m. - Sudeepta: Its damn hard to score consistent 1s and 2s if the threat of big shot isn't present. If I captain against a player without a threat of big shots I will ask everyone in the inner circle to step in and boundary riders to be 5 yards in. Good luck with 6 1s and 2s per over then.
2025-09-18, 9:54 a.m. - Sudeepta: and it doesn't have to be 2 boundaries and 4 dots, you can hit 2 boundaries and still get singles
2025-09-18, 10:08 a.m. - Pritam Da: The Easiest way to bypass this problem for me is to be prepared with one "get out of jail" big shot. 

For example, a slog sweep, or hoik over mid wicket, or down the wicket over long on, or slash over cover or cover point etc... Whatever works for that particular batsman, the batsman needs to develop that shot.

Sometimes the intent/attempt of that shot spreads the field which allows those 1's & 2's to open up. However, if not fit to run hard for those 2's, then unfortunately  its counter productiv.
2025-09-18, 10:49 a.m. - Sudeepta: I think thats the wrong mindset. When you say get out of jail free it implies that it is out of the norm to go for boundaries. That should not be the case, looking for boundaries should be the default
2025-09-18, 10:49 a.m. - Sharif Bhai: I am not saying a team should only play singles and twos. As per the article being aggressive is not the only answer. Pakistan often plays with aggression but the real challenge is to balance it with smart risk management.

When the game slows down you still have to keep the scoreboard moving. Two boundaries with four dot balls give the same result as one boundary with four singles. The key is to keep the runs coming manage the risk and know when to push instead of swinging hard at every ball

Whyte Ave
Assalamu Alaikum wa Rahmatullah!
.. .
X
We are looking for a male, Muslim roommate to move in starting October 2025 in the Whyte Ave area. Preference will be given to students or young professionals.
The apartment has two bedrooms, with the living room converted into a bedroom available for rent at an affordable cost of C$400, plus utilities (C$45-65).
The location (106 st, 83 Ave) is conveniently close to multiple bus stops (only a 2-3 minute walk) and approximately a 16-minute walk to the University of Alberta North Campus. Also, it is near multiple grocery stores, halal restaurants, mosques, churches, the Edmonton Public Library, and the Strathcona Farmer's Market.
Some furniture and winter accessories, e.g., tables, chairs, boots, and jackets, are also available for buying at a minimal price.
If you are interested, we would be happy to discuss further details and arrange an in-person or virtual tour of the apartment.
Thank you!

2025-11-01, 7:39 p.m. - Nazmus Sakeef: Hello Vai brothers,

Assalamu Alaikum.

Hope everyone’s doing well and enjoying the break, getting some much-needed rest before the new season begins.

We’re happy to share that indoor practice for the upcoming season will start on November 23rd, every Sunday from 7:00 PM to 9:00 PM at Govind’s facility (both nets booked). Let’s get back into rhythm and gear up for another exciting season ahead!

Also, just a quick update — as per our RBCC bylaws, October 31st marks the official end of RBCC’s fiscal year (2024–2025). Following our Award & Volunteer Appreciation Night, we will be conducting the election for the new RBCC Executive Committee on 22nd November (Tentative)

!!*IMPORTANT *
Lastly, the current executive team has decided *not to continue* for the next season. Anyone's participation and enthusiasm will be really meaningful for RBCC's upcoming season.

Thank you all for your continued support. Let’s keep the RBCC spirit alive — on and off the field. 

#RBCC Executive 2025

2025-11-15, 6:19 p.m. - Anamul: 📢 RBCC Election Nomination Announcement

Hello Everyone,

It is that time of the year again. This is your chance to step forward, contribute, and help shape the future of RBCC.

We are opening nominations for all Executive Team positions as well as the Common Selector position and the Disciplinary Committee for upcoming season 2026

Positions open for nomination:
	•	President
	•	VP Finance
	•	VP Admin
	•	VP Internal Affairs
	•	VP Communications
	•	Common Selector
	•	Disciplinary Committee

You will have a 7-day nomination period, followed by 2 days for withdrawal.

Nomination end date: 22nd November, 11:59pm

Election day: 25th November

If you feel passionate about strengthening the club, or simply want to give back to the RBCC community, this is your moment to step up. Please send your nominations directly to VP Admin (Anamul).

For eligibility criteria for voting, kindly refer to the bylaws attached.

Thank you and best of luck to all nominees.

Best Regards,
Anamul

2025-11-30, 4:25 p.m. - Sharif Bhai: Partially true. A by-law on its own may not give any extra benefit. But if you want to play cricket in a league, you need people, money, and a responsible plan for how to raise and spend that money.

2026-01-13, 9:17 a.m. - Sharif Bhai: To put it very bluntly, it is widely known that our club has differences of opinion, and it always has. This is not a problem. 

What truly matters is that we remain focused on our shared goal: building a stronger future for the club, both financially and organizationally. 

Achieving this requires patience with one another and a willingness to compromise. Small disagreements and verbal clashes do not matter in the bigger picture. Like a car, the club can only move forward if all its wheels are turning in the same direction.

Peace or chaos is a choice we all have to make.
2026-01-13, 9:30 a.m. - Sharif Bhai: Executives, in particular, must work together and make decisions as a group. No decision should be made without proper discussion, and none should be influenced by outside pressure. 

If a decision takes time, that is acceptable. What matters is that when it is communicated, it reflects the executive team as a whole. 

Even when there are disagreements, the final decision should be unified and clearly thought through.

2026-01-16, 6:16 p.m. - JD: Hey @everyone, 

I wanted to address something that's been brought to my attention. 

Some of you feel I've been too direct, that I speak too loudly, that I don't give space for others to contribute. If any of you feel that way, I'm sorry. That's never been my intent.

I've been with this club almost six years. Most of that time, I stayed quiet. This is probably the first year you've seen me speak up this much outside the field. 

I'm not naturally a talker. I know I have limitations when it comes to communication. I get worked up when speaking, which is why I've always tried to speak less.

But here's what I want to clarify: I don't fight for myself. I never have, never will. 

When I raise my voice, it's always about fairness, about making sure everyone gets heard, about fixing something that doesn't sit right. Never about me personally.

Everything I do is an internal battle. I don't compete against anyone. From struggling to speak properly growing up, stuttering, dyslexia, working in environments where my accent stood out, mumbling—these are things I've worked hard to overcome. Still working on them. It's an ongoing process. 

When I joined army, lot of the classes & training were in English even though I only ever studied in French. I had an accent, easy target. People made fun of how I spoke, mocked me. But some guys helped me anyway. They laughed at me, corrected me, and I got better because of it. If they never would have pointed out my mistakes, I'd have never improved.

That's how I approach things in life too. If I'm doing something wrong, tell me. I want to know. 

I want different opinions. 

That's what makes us stronger. 

Disagreement doesn't mean disrespect—it just means we're looking at things from different perspectives.

My battles should stay within my boundaries. But during battles, unfortunately, war debris fall on unwanted territories. 

If my battles have casted shadows into your boundaries, if my tone has crossed a line, if I've made anyone feel unheard, I apologize. 

I'll work on it. I hold myself to a higher standard, and I'll make sure I'm more mindful going forward.

No one asked me to write this nor apologize. I'm doing it because I believe we should be honest with each other & for my sanity.

That's the only way this club gets better.

Thank you for your putting up with me and for bringing this to my attention. Let's keep building something great together.

2026-01-16, 8:28 p.m. - Sharif Bhai: That tells me not to bother with what others think about you. Give your honest opinions, and if they’re ignored, that’s other people’s loss, not yours. You move on to the next. You are what you think you are.
2026-01-16, 10:33 p.m. - Simon Bhai Tanveer Ahmed: @⁨JD⁩: My two cents- when people do not express their thoughts, emotions build up like inside a pressure cooker. You run your thoughts in your mind again and again so many times that it feels like "common sense" to you. And you think it is "common sense" for everyone, which actually is not.
So when you see that your percieved common sense is not being acted on, it frustrates you. And at some point, everything comes out as an angry outburst.

It happens to most introvert people in various aspects of life. So best practice is to start expressing thoughts more frequently. That way you will sound less angry and feel less frustrated. 

Communication is a skill to practice. And what place can be better to practice than here. Just be prepared and open to apologise frequently for any mistakes. Look at Sid, even he is less angry these days 😛

Also, it might help with running between the wickets as well 🤞
2026-01-16, 11:08 p.m. - JD: I miss you, @⁨Simon Bhai Tanveer Ahmed⁩ bhai. Even in our short interactions, I've learned so much from you.

If I could articulate my thoughts, I wonder what I might be capable of. Unfortunately, I have no control over them. It bounces from one thing to another. (I call it shiny object syndrome, picked it up from some random reel)

My anger and frustration don't come from pressure buildup but rather a reaction to recent events.

Oh, I am getting a bit too much of verbal communication practice these days. Well, I am being forced to; it's getting me out of my comfort zone for sure.

We can never learn enough. Being able to communicate concisely is a skill I'm focusing on, but the more I learn, the less I want to speak. 

Because my monkey brain can't synthesize my thoughts. <This message was edited>
2026-01-17, 12:50 a.m. - Simon Bhai Tanveer Ahmed: We all have our stuggles in different shapes and forms. No one, not even the happiest looking one is out of struggle. Its just different for each. So, if we can empathize for people, we can let go of small things, like minor miscommunications, or a bad day on field. End of the day, in sports you do not save lives. In workfields you do. So take it on the entertainment side, allow to make mistakes and do that yourselves. And then fight, next forgive, then forget and afterwards build the next innings together. Thats all sports.

Also, a lump of gold needs to go through heat and pressure to melt and then reshaped to the beauty. Dont stop at pain, keep trying man. 

Also, I am always there for anyone, just a phone call away, u know! Love you team, love and respect for all the personal battles.

2026-01-21, 10:05 a.m. - Sharif Bhai: “This room knows, this is classic risk management — risk management comes at a price. But that cost of strategic autonomy — of sovereignty — can also be shared. Collective investments in resilience are cheaper than everyone building their own fortresses. Shared standards reduce fragmentation. Complementarities are positive sum…

And our new approach rests on what Alexander Stubb has termed "values-based realism" — or, to put another way, *we aim to be principled and pragmatic*. 

Principled in our commitment to fundamental values: sovereignty and territorial integrity, the prohibition of the use of force except when consistent with the UN Charter and respect for human rights.

And pragmatic in recognizing that progress is often incremental, that interests diverge, that not every partner will share our values. So we're engaging broadly, strategically, with open eyes. We actively take on the world as it is, not wait around for a world we wish to be.

Mark Carney <This message was edited>

Dear RBCC Members,

We warmly invite all active, paid members of Royal Bengal Cricket Club to attend our Annual General Meeting (AGM) on Saturday, January 31, 2026, at Malmo Community League, from 12:00 PM to 5:00 PM.

We believe most of you have already received the official AGM email sent to your personal email addresses on January 10 (*as per bylaws 21 days earlier than AGM*).Thank you to those who have already shared their suggested edits and feedback via email — your engagement and input are truly appreciated.

Looking forward to seeing you all and having a constructive, positive discussion together.

Thank you and see you there.
— RBCC Executive Team

2026-02-07, 11:04 a.m. - Faiaz: ⚠️ Important Announcement ⚠️ 

As the executive team, we have started planning for the upcoming season. We want to provide our vision so that club members can provide their input. This is also subject to discussions with the selected captains and the vision they would have.

We aim to have 4 teams, A and B teams in two formats (20 and 40 overs). There will be synergy for the A (high performance) and B (developmental) team to work together. Strong performances in the B team in one particular format will open the door to A team selection in that same format, ensuring players are rewarded directly for their efforts, and players dropped from A team due to lack of form will play in B team to regain their place.

Ultimately, in both formats, our goal would be to put the best team forward for each match and to have clear pathways for each player to play for either team, according to their preference.

As a first step, we want to gauge interest among this upcoming season's players regarding their interest and availability. Please respond to the poll below (select as many options as you prefer).
2026-02-07, 11:06 a.m. - Faiaz: POLL:
What is your expected availability to play in the upcoming season (assuming you are selected, you would play at least 90% of the time)?
OPTION: A team 40 overs (weekends) (19 votes)
OPTION: B team 40 overs (weekends) (17 votes)
OPTION: A team 20 overs (weekdays) (23 votes)
OPTION: B team 20 overs (weekdays) (19 votes)
OPTION: Not playing this season (6 votes)

2026-02-07, 11:07 a.m. - Faiaz: Deadline for the poll: Tuesday, Feb 10th, 11 am. Please respond, this will help us to plan the season tremendously. Thank you,
Exec team.
